coredata.dc:title: Förster resonance energy transfer-based molecularly imprinted polymer /amine-functionalized metal-organic framework nanocomposite for trace level detection of 4-nitrophenol
coredata.dc:creator: Amiripour, Fatemeh
Ghasemi, Shahram
Azizi, Seyed Naser
coredata.dc:description: Herein, luminescent metal-organic framework (LMOF) is modified with molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) and employed as an efficient and cost-effective nanoprobe for the determination of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP). Amine-UiO-66 support with unique luminescence property is chosen for creating the template of 4-NP using MIP to construct a highly selective LMOF-based sensor (amine-UiO-66/MIP nanocomposite). The imprinted sites formed at the surface of LMOF significantly enhance the selectivity of the probe for 4-NP over its analogs. 4-NP can effectively quench the fluorescent intensity of the optical sensor via the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. Amine-UiO-66/MIP sensor provided the linear range of 0.05–50 μM and low limit of detection of 0.009 μM for sensing of 4-NP. The application of probe for the fluorometric analysis of real samples, including drinking and environmental water, provided satisfactory recovery results with low relative standard deviation (RSD).

(C) Schematic illustration of the amine-UiO-66/MIP sensor for 4-NP detection. The effect of (D) pH and (E) incubation time on the fluorescence intensity in the presence of analyte.

head.ce:title: Förster resonance energy transfer-based molecularly imprinted polymer /amine-functionalized metal-organic framework nanocomposite for trace level detection of 4-nitrophenol

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, luminescent metal-organic framework (LMOF) is modified with molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) and employed as an efficient and cost-effective nanoprobe for the determination of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP). Amine-UiO-66 support with unique luminescence property is chosen for creating the template of 4-NP using MIP to construct a highly selective LMOF-based sensor (amine-UiO-66/MIP nanocomposite). The imprinted sites formed at the surface of LMOF significantly enhance the selectivity of the probe for 4-NP over its analogs. 4-NP can effectively quench the fluorescent intensity of the optical sensor via the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. Amine-UiO-66/MIP sensor provided the linear range of 0.05–50 μM and low limit of detection of 0.009 μM for sensing of 4-NP. The application of probe for the fluorometric analysis of real samples, including drinking and environmental water, provided satisfactory recovery results with low relative standard deviation (RSD).

ce:section.ce:para: As a well-known nitro-aromatic compound (NACs), 4-Nitrophenol (4-NP) has been used extensively in pesticide production as well as medical and dye industries [1,2]. 4-NP is also known as an environmentally harmful compound and threatens human safety which causes to headache and injuries to the central nervous system [3–5]. Owing to its toxicity and mutagenicity effects as well as poor biodegradability, the maximum allowed 4-NP concentration in drinking water was set to 60 ppm (∼0.43 μM) by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) [6,7]. So, there is a very urgent need for the determination or separation of trace amounts of 4-NP in water samples in any way possible. Currently, the detection strategies for trace value of 4-NP include electrochemical methods [8–10], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry [11], and spectrofluorimetry [12]. Some of these methods have high sensitivity and anti-interference ability, but the requirement of complicated analytical procedures and expert operators are known as the bottleneck of these methods for analytical applications [13,14]. Contrarily, in recent years, fluorescence method offers one of the fastest ways for its rapid responsiveness, low limit of detection, high sensitivity, and selectivity [15]. Currently, several sensing strategies for the measurement of 4-NP have been designed based on the fluorescence methods [4,16–25].

Metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) with intrinsic luminescence have been considered for fluorescence detection of organic analytes and metal ions [25]. Gas storage/separation [26,27], catalysis and photocatalysis [28–30], toxic compound adsorption [31,32], sensing [33–35], and electrochemical applications [36] are some areas in which MOFs have been widely investigated. Thanks to their exceptional properties, such as the superior surface area and accessible pore volume, inherent aperture, adjustable porosity, and suitable chemical and thermal stabilities [25], MOFs are applied to prepare efficacious fluorescent sensors. However, despite the advances in developing of such highly sensitive analytical sensors for specific recognition of NACs, luminescent MOFs (LMOFs) encounter some problems in measuring discernible signal changes. 2-NP and 3-NP are structural analogous to 4-NP, which interfere with its detection, and LMOFs cannot produce meaningful selective responses to NACs. In this context, the creation of selective recognition sites for 4-NP on LMOF surface through the imprinting process can enable development of the sensors with specific receptors for highly selective detection of NACs [4,22,37].

In molecular imprinting technology, polymers with recognition sites complementary in shape and cavities with the targets are formed. The preparation of fluorescent molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) using gold nanoclusters [38], semiconductor quantum dots [39–41], graphene quantum dots [4,42], and carbon quantum dots [43,44], are of interest for optical sensing. The basic concept of MIP preparation is quite simple, relying on two steps: 1) the template molecules link to functional monomers to form a complex; 2) the removal of target molecules from the MIP layer by several elution steps to create custom-fit recognition characters. The empty selective molecular cavities formed in the MIP created on or near the surface of the carrier can selectively recombine with the target molecules for the determination of an analyte in solutions.

MOF-based sensors, including optical [45] or electrochemical [46], as well as quartz crystal microbalance sensor [47], use MIPs with synthetic receptors as recognition sites to boost their sensing performance. The presence of MOFs can promote the number of imprinted sites and the performance of the sensor in the determination of analyte in comparison to MIP itself. Therefore, the formation of MIP to produce molecular receptors at the surface of MOF offers a promising approach that can skillfully be applied in the analytical field.

Based on the above considerations, an innovative and effective optical sensor utilizing the modification of LMOF surface with MIP was constructed for the determination of 4-NP. Herein, a blue florescent amine-functionalized-LMOF (NH2-UiO-66) with inherent emission maximum at 428 nm was synthesized and then, covered by MIP during post-synthetic modification (PSM) process (amine-UiO-66/MIP) to construct sensitive and selective sensor toward trace amounts of 4-NP. MIP on the surface of amine-UiO-66 provides the recognition sites for specific adsorption of 4-NP. The amine-UiO-66 acts as the porous support and fluorescence indicator to sense the bonding interaction events and produce the fluorescence intensity relating to the presence of 4-NP.

Despite that, some sensors based on the combination of the advantages MOF and MIP were constructed, however rare of them are devoted to the modification of Zr-LMOFs surface with MIP. To the best our knowledge, no report has been focused on amine-UiO-66/MIP for fluorometric detection of 4-NP. The amine-UiO-66/MIP probe combines the benefits of MIP and amine-UiO-66 to provide a linear response in the concentration region of 0.05–50 μM with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.009 μM for rapid, sensitive, and selective sensing of 4-NP. The results also showed that the amine-UiO-66/MIP sensor could selectively distinguish 4-NP in the presence of other NACs, which makes it suitable for real samples analysis.
All of the reagents were used without further purification. The information about the reagents and chemicals is presented in the Supplementary material.

Amine-UiO-66-NH2 was prepared by solvothermal method [48], in which the precursors including zirconium chloride and 2-NH2-terephthalic acid (ATA) in N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) with specific molar ratio were mixed. In this regard, firstly, 0.2332 g of ZrCl4 was added in DMF (20 mL) with continuous stirring at room temperature (solution 1). In parallel, solution 2 comprising the 0.1812 g of ATA in 20 mL of DMF was prepared. At the next step, solution 2 was poured into solution 1, and immediately, 19.8 mL of acetic acid as a modulator was added to the mixture following by the stirring for 15 min. Then, it was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 48 h in an oven. After this time, the formed particles were separated via centrifugation and rinsed with methanol several times.

Amine-UiO-66/MIP was prepared via the PSM method. The amine-UiO-66 was dispersed in 35 mL of methanol. Then, 1 mmol of 4-NP as template molecule, 168 μL of monomer methacrylic acid (MAA), and 1.88 mL cross-linker of ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) were injected into the solution under stirring. After 15 min, the polymerization initiator of 2,2-azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) was introduced to the mixture and maintained for 15 min under N2 gas pressure. The solution was transferred to a flask and heated at 60 °C for 1 day. After the polymerization, the white solid was rinsed several times and heated in a vacuum oven for further use. The preparation steps are shown schematically in Fig. 1 A. A non-imprinted polymer-coated amine-UiO-66 (amine-UiO-66/NIP) was also prepared according to the same procedure without the template.

150 mg of amine-UiO-66/MIP was dispersed in 100 mL of phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 8.0) and sonicated for 2 h, and then, 3 mL of the solution was transferred to a quartz cuvette. To introduce the detection capability of amine-UiO-66/MIP by fluorescence, the specific amounts of standard 4-NP solutions in successive addition were injected into the above solution to obtain the final concentrations in the desired range. All the injected solutions were sonicated for 5 min before fluorimetric detection. The fluorescence experiments were conducted at ambient temperature at the excitation wavelengths of 340 nm, while the slit width was adjusted at 10 nm.

Real samples experiments were used to evaluate the accuracy and applicability of amine-UiO-66/MIP in sensing of 4-NP. Two real samples (drinking and river water) were provided from our laboratory and Babol River (Babolsar, Iran). Firstly, the samples were centrifuged at 7000 rpm and the upper solutions were collected and filtrated by the 0.22 μm membrane to remove any suspended particles. Specific amounts of 4-NP were spiked to the pre-treated solutions, and then, 300 μL of spiked solutions were added to 3 mL of the probe solution (1.5 g L−1). After the optimum incubation time (5 min), the fluorescent intensities of the mixture were recorded at excitation wavelength of 340 nm.

As illustrated in Fig. 1 A, to enhance the selectivity and sensitivity of the probe-based LMOF, MIP film is coated at the surface of amine-UiO-66 via polymerization reaction in the presence of template (4-NP), functional monomer (MAA), cross-linker (EGDMA), and initiator (AIBN).

In X-ray diffraction (XRD, by Bruker D8 diffractometer with CuKα radiation) pattern of amine-UiO-66 (Fig. 1 B), the characteristic peaks at 2θ of 7 and 8° are related to the (111) and (200) crystal planes, respectively [49,50,51]. The XRD pattern of amine-UiO-66/MIP composite is also shown in Fig. 1 B. It can be seen that there are no significant changes between the XRD patterns of the two samples, demonstrating the stability of amine-UiO-66 during the synthesis.

In Fourier-transform infrared spectrum (FT-IR, by AVATAR spectrometer) of amine-UiO-66, the bands of –NH2 (3235 and 3044 cm−1), Zr–O (483 cm−1), –OCO (1430 cm−1) and CC (1400-1500 cm−1) are observed (Fig. 1 C-a). In addition, the characteristic absorption peaks at 1670, 1522 and 1410 cm−1 in amine-UiO-66/MIP are assigned to the stretching vibrations of C–O, N–H and C–N, respectively [50,51,52]. In spectrum of amine-UiO-66/MIP (Fig. 1 C-b), the Zr–O vibration is in the same region as amine-UiO-66 (Fig. 1 C-a), which confirms the presence of amine-UiO-66 in the composite. The absorption bands of amine-UiO-66/MIP and amine-UiO-66 are nearly similar, which indicate the structure of the LMOF reserved during the coating process with MIP, and the imprinted layer was formed successfully. Some peaks in the FT-IR spectrum of amine-UiO-66/MIP shift toward higher wavenumbers, which may be due to weak electrostatic interaction or hydrogen bonding of MIP to functional groups of amine-UiO-66.

The characterization of amine-UiO-66 using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, by MIRA3, TESCAN) shows the formation of irregular shape particles, which are composed of nanometer-sized particles (Fig. 2 A and B). According to the images, the modification of the amine-UiO-66 surface by MIP leads to insignificant changes in the morphology of the particles. However, some degree of agglomeration of amine-UiO-66 nanoparticles is observed (Fig. 2 C and D). Using the transmission electron microscope (TEM, by Philips CM30), the average dimension of amine-UiO-66/MIP nanoparticles is estimated about 40–60 nm (Fig. 3 A and B).

The energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS, by MIRA3, TESCAN) technique was used to investigate the presence of elements in the samples. The characteristic Kα and Kβ lines of C, N, O, and Zr elements are present in EDS spectra of amine-UiO-66 (Fig. S1 A) and amine-UiO-66/MIP (Fig. 3C) with different intensities. In addition, in EDS spectrum of MIP, the characteristic Kα of C and O elements are observed (Fig. S1 B). The values of atomic percent (A %) for different elements are given in Table 1. From elemental mapping analysis, the distribution of elements for amine-UiO-66/MIP nanocomposite is homogenous (Fig. 3 D and E), which is important for the performance of probe in practical applications.

The specific surface area and total porosity of samples have significant effect in improving the performance of the typical probe. The probe with high surface area reduces the analyte mass transfer resistance at the surface and promotes the sensitivity for the analyte detection. Brunauer -Emmett –Teller (BET) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) methods were used to calculate the specific surface area and total porosity of the samples (Fig. 4 ). Nitrogen adsorption/desorption plot for amine-UiO-66 sample (via BELSORP mini II instrument) shows type I isotherm, which is a prominent feature of microporous compounds with high structural order (Fig. 4 A) [53]. For amine-UiO-66, BET surface area is calculated 895.95 m2 g−1 with the total porosity volume of 0.40 cm3 g−1 and average pore diameter of 1.80 nm (Fig. 4 B), which is near to the value reported in previous works [54,55].

The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of amine-UiO-66/MIP and MIP are shown in Fig. 4 C and E. The isotherms present the type IV behavior, which are related to mesoporous materials [53]. Higher isothermal slope of the amine-UiO-66/MIP composite compared to MIP indicates that amine-UiO-66/MIP has more uniform shape and size cavities than MIP. In addition, larger volume of cavities for amine-UiO-66/MIP than MIP can be deduced from higher height of the amine-UiO-66/MIP isotherm. From BJH plots (Fig. 4 D and F), the average pore sizes for MIP and amine-UiO-66/MIP are also obtained 2.21 and 1.98 nm, respectively. The parameters including surface area, total pores volume, and average pores diameter were obtained from physical N2 adsorption/desorption and BET analysis are reported in Table 2 . The surface area and total pores volume related to amine-UiO-66/MIP nanocomposite are equal to 823.78 m2 g−1 and 0.38 cm3 g−1, respectively. The values are slightly lower than that for amine-UiO-66 and are higher than that for MIP. It seems that occupying and blocking of the pores in amine-UiO-66 as a result of coating with MIP are the reason for reducing the surface area and the total pores volume of amine-UiO-66/MIP nanocomposite. On the other hand, MIP has low total porosity compared to amine-UiO-66 and amine-UiO-66/MIP. When it was coated on amine-UiO-66, the corresponding surface area and total porosity enhance which is helpful for more adsorption of 4-NP to the surface.

In order to evaluate the role of different materials in the fluorescent probe, a series of experiments was designed using MIP, amine-UiO-66, and amine-UiO-66/NIP as control samples. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensities of amine-UiO-66/MIP and amine-UiO-66 decrease in the presence of 4-NP (50 μM) with different quenching degree (F0/F, where F0 and F defined as the intensities of the probe in the absence and presence of 50 μM of the analyte, respectively). The amine-UiO-66 exhibits the good quenching effect, but not as much as amine-UiO-66/MIP. From Fig. 5 A, the F0/F of amine-UiO-66 is calculated to be ∼3.92, which is ∼1.54 times lower than that of amine-UiO-66/MIP (∼6). The adsorption capacity of amine-UiO-66 improves significantly with MIP. The superior response of amine-UiO-66/MIP nanocomposite is attributed to the synthetic molecular receptors with specific recognition sites for 4-NP, which is created during the polymerization of MIP onto the surface of amine-UiO-66. The MIP layer can selectively recognize and bind 4-NP back into their tailor-made recognition sites with the significant quench in the fluorescence of amine-UiO-66/MIP. In contrast, MIP has not any fluorescence response toward 4-NP. This reveals that amine-UiO-66 is a beneficial support in improving the recognition ability of the MIP. It can increase the total specific surface area due to its porous structure, which reduces the mass transfer resistance for 4-NP at the surface of the probe and causes to effective fluorescence quenching for the determination of the analyte. For amine-UiO-66/NIP, irregular molecular cavities are created during polymerization onto the surface of amine-UiO-66 without the 4-NP template molecule, so low florescent quenching was observed for amine-UiO-66/NIP toward the addition of 4-NP. However, amine-UiO-66/NIP still displays a certain degree of fluorescent selectivity to 4-NP than traditional non-imprinted materials, which originates from amine-UiO-66 itself.

The quenching in the fluorescence of amine-UiO-66/MIP nanocomposite induced by 4-NP can be discussed by investigating the interaction between them. Fig. 5 B shows the excitation spectrum of amine-UiO-66/MIP dispersed in PBS (1.5 mg L−1) before and after interaction with 4-NP. In the spectrum of amine-UiO-66/MIP, the band at 260 nm originates from the π-π∗ electronic transition in ATA and the absorption of Zr-oxo cluster and the band at 330 nm is related to the n-π∗ charge transfer of the –NH2 in ligand (Fig. 5 B) [56]. As can be seen, the excitation spectrum of amine-UiO-66/MIP after interaction with 4-NP can be in accordance with the stacked excitation spectrum of 4-NP and amine-UiO-66/MIP, meanwhile there is no obvious new peaks to mark the complexe formation. So, it can be concluded that no real interaction such as complex formation between amine-UiO-66/MIP and 4-NP has occurred, and in other words, the interaction between 4-NP and amine-UiO-66/MIP is extremely weak. Meanwhile, the absorption spectrum of 4-NP overlaps with the emission spectrum of amine-UiO-66/MIP, which suggests the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism is included in the quenching of fluorescence intensity. FRET is based on non-radiative dipole–dipole coupling and involves the spectral overlap between the donor emission and the acceptor absorption spectra [57]. In this way, the fluorescence quenching of amine-UiO-66/MIP in the presence of 4-NP indicates the FRET from amine-UiO-66/MIP (donor) to 4-NP (acceptor) (as shown in Fig. 5 C). The FRET efficiency depends on the distance separating donor and acceptor. The ternary interactions of 4-NP, MIP, and UiO-66-NH2 become more complex when all three components are present in the solution. The surface coating of the LMOF is a parameter that should be taken into account, as it can affect the distance between the donor-acceptor pair and the ability of the probe to bind to another molecule [58,59]. MIP may be act as a moderator for amine-UiO-66 quenching by 4-NP [60]. In addition, the size of pores in the MOF structure or on its surface as well as the size and position of MIP film may be involved in the FRET process.

The experimental conditions were investigated to achieve a high sensing performance. At first, the pH of the solution was investigated on the determination of 4-NP by the probe. The quenching efficiency is highly pH-dependent, and the results show the best performance at pH = 8 for 4-NP (Fig. 5 D). Due to the fact that 4-NP has a negative charge at alkaline pH (pKa = 7.08), the efficiency of amine-UiO-66/MIP decreases due to the repulsion between its negative charge and the sensor surface. At lower pH, 4-NP is present in its molecular form in the environment and the decrease in sensor efficiency may be due to the protonation of nitrogen in the LMOF structure.

Response time is another important key factor in practical applications. The suitable signal for 4-NP adsorption and signal transduction by amine-UiO-66/MIP is obtained in 5 min (Fig. 5 E). This time is fast enough for the on-site determination of 4-NP, which arises from the mesoporous structure and high surface area of amine-UiO-66/MIP. The decrease of quenching efficiency in times lower than 5 min may be due to empty sites at the surface of amine-UiO-66/MIP. At times higher than 5 min, F0/F remains almost constant, which contributes to the filling of surface capacity for 4-NP adsorption. Therefore, the incubation time of 5 min at pH = 8 was selected for further studies.

The fluorescence titration was performed upon the addition of different concentrations of 4-NP to the solution of florescent amine-UiO-66/MIP. The results showed the dependence of fluorescence response (F0/F) with the dose of 4-NP in the solution. As presented in Fig. 6 A, the fluorescence intensity of amine-UiO-66/MIP quenches gradually with increasing the 4-NP concentration over the range of 0.05–50 μM. The Ksv value of 0.088 μM−1 is obtained via the fitted linear regression curve with a correlation coefficient of 0.998 (Fig. 6 B) to Stern-Volmer equation [61]:
F 0
/ F =

where C defines the known concentration of the analyte.

From the standard deviation (SD) of blank solution (Sb) and slope of the calibration curve (m), the limit of detection using 3Sb/m is estimated as low as 0.009 μM, which is lower than that defined by UEPA for the permitted value of 4-NP in drinking water (∼0.43 μM) [62]. To further confirm the selectivity of proposed sensor, imprinting factor (IF = KSV, amine-UiO-66/MIP/KSV, amine-UiO-66/NIP) was calculated. Imprinting factor is a measure of the strength of interaction of the imprinted polymer towards the template molecule, calculated by the ratio of the KSV obtained for amine-UiO-66/MIP and amine-UiO-66/NIP. For this aim, the fluorescence responses of amine-UiO-66/NIP toward the addition of 4-NP are recorded (Fig. 6 C) and the related calibration curve is plotted in Fig. 6 D. As can be seen, the KSV value of amine-UiO-66/NIP decreases relative to that obtained for amine-UiO-66/MIP. The proposed sensor has IF = 9.2, which shows the vital role of imprinting sites in adsorption of 4-NP.

In addition, the performance of sensor is compared to other reported probes for 4-NP detection in analytical parameters, including sensitivity, detection limit, and linear range (Table 3 ). The results signify that the developed method based on amine-UiO-66/MIP acquires favorable detection parameters among the other sensors, despite the simple operation process. The high sensitivity of amine-UiO-66/MIP sensor arises from combining the superior fluorescence intensity of porous LMOF and more adsorption of 4-NP on MIP.

Subsequently, selectivity and anti-interference ability are important parameter for evaluating the sensing performance in the complicated matrix. NACs including 3-nitrophenol (3-NP), 2-nitrophenol (2-NP), and also, similar phenolic compounds such as phenol (PHE), trinitrophenol (TNP), and dinitrophenol (DNP) have similar structures, which make the challenge for measurement of 4-NP in real samples.

The investigations showed that the 4-NP induced much higher degree of fluorescence quenching (F0/F) of amine-UiO-66/MIP (∼6) than nitrophenol analogs (<2.1) with the same concentration (50 μM). The fluorescence responses of amine-UiO-66/MIP to TNP and DNP are slightly higher than 3-NP, 2-NP, and PHE (Fig. 7 A), which are attributed to the great affinity of electron-deficient NACs to the of amine group in amine-UiO-66, although this quenching is insignificant. Further access to demonstrate the capability of the probe in detecting 4-NP is performed via the calculating the IF for 4-NP analogs (Fig. S2). The IF values are 1.66, 0.82, 2.15, 0.69, and 1.26 for DNP, TNP, PHE, 2-NP, and 3-NP, respectively which are equally 4 times lower than that for 4-NP. The analogs could not effectively enter the cavities on the surface of the sensor to induce fluorescence quenching.

In addition, we have not found notable quenching in fluorescent of amine-UiO-66/MIP by some cations with the concentration of 50-fold ((F0/F) is equal to 1.5) (Fig. 7 A). Based on these results, the superior selectivity of amine-UiO-66/MIP in the determination of 4-NP despite the presence of other interference species can be explored.

The comparative selectivity tests were performed with the addition of different phenolic compounds (at the same concentration) to the amine-UiO-66 solution and recording the related fluorescent responses (Fig. 7 B). As shown, TNP represents the highest quenching effect (∼6) on the fluorescent of amine-UiO-66 relative to 4-NP, 3-NP, 2-NP, and PHE. This demonstrates the excellent role of imprinted sites for 4-NP in enhancing the detection capability of amine-UiO-66/MIP.

To investigate the short-time stability of amine-UiO-66/MIP nanocomposite, successive determinations of 4-NP (seven times during a day) with the concentration of 50 μM was performed, and quite well stability (SD below 3.0 %) was achieved. In parallel, experiments on intermediate precision (inter-day) detection over six days indicate the enough stability of the proposed method for 4-NP determination (50 μM) with SD (lower than 3.0%) in real applications.

The reusability of amine-UiO-66/MIP for 4-NP (50 μM) was also evaluated. The sensing ability of the probe was explored for three cycles of adsorption/desorption. Following the sensing experiment, the dispersed probe was separated via ultracentrifugation and then washed several times with pure methanol. The solid immersed in pure ethanol for 3 h and finally dried in an oven at 60 °C for 10 h. In some literatures, 0.5 M NaOH and 0.2 M glycine have been used for reusing the probe [75,76]. In this work, we used pure ethanol to refresh the sites of probe. To evaluate the reusability of the proposed probe, sensing experiments were carried out using 50 μM of 4-NP. It was found that, after reusing three times (Fig. S3), the probe shows the quenching degree of 5.6, which is almost near to that for the fresh one. This revealed the recyclability sensing property of amine-UiO-66/MIP, which offers cost-effectiveness and efficiency of senor for fluorimetric detection of 4-NP.

To evaluate the application of the suggested method, amine-UiO-66/MIP was utilized to monitor and detect the 4-NP in drinking and river water samples via the quenching effect. In all of the above samples, 4-NP was not found by the amine-UiO-66/MIP sensor and, so, they were spiked with the certain amount of 4-NP standard solution. To determine the accuracy of method, recovery rates for spiked samples are calculated, and the results are provided in Table 4 . The obtained recoveries are in the agreeable range of 97–105%, which indicates the great potential of amine-UiO-66/MIP for fluorimetric sensing of 4-NP in the complicated matrix.

In conclusion, we designed a sensor for 4-NP measurement by fluorometric method based on the surface LMOF modification via molecular imprinting technique. The evaluation of the probe performance exhibited the emission quenching of amine-UiO-66/MIP nanocomposite in the presence of trace level of 4-NP which is much higher than UiO-66-NH2 and MIP. It is confirmed that the presence of UiO-66-NH2 in the probe architecture can increase the detection sites via the enhancing of the amine-UiO-66/MIP surface area. The formation of the MIP on the sensor surface is also effective in absorbing more of the target molecule due to the formation of cavities consistent with the size and shape of the 4-NP molecule. Through the analytical tests, low LOD(0.009 μM) and good linear relationship (0.05–50 μM) with quick response were obtained for 4-NP. This sensor can selectively detect 4-NP in buffer solution among other NACs (2-NP and 3-NP) as well as other aromatic compounds (PHE, DNP, and TNP), while it is not detected by the UiO-66-NH2. Furthermore, the amount of fluorescence quenching is unchanged after 3 times of use, enabling the reuse of amine-UiO-66/MIP for 4-NP detection. Notably, the probe was successfully investigated for sensing of spiked 4-NP in real samples and the results demonstrate the potential capability of amine-UiO-66/MIP for developing new fluorescent material with high sensitivity and selectivity for various target molecules.

Fatemeh Amiripour: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Shahram Ghasemi: Supervision, Validation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Seyed Naser Azizi: Supervision, Conceptualization, Validation, Visualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Methodology.
